title: Breathe with Ana
summary: Backstage, a retired conductor gives a twenty-four-year-old violinist two minutes of advice before her first concert in a major German orchestra.

body.speaker: Hartwig
body.text: Mia. Two minutes. They will call you.

body.speaker: Mia
body.text: Maestro, you came.

body.speaker: Hartwig
body.text: I came. I have not been backstage in three years.

body.speaker: Mia
body.text: I am terrified.

body.speaker: Hartwig
body.text: Good. Tell me what you are afraid of.

body.speaker: Mia
body.text: Forgetting where I am. The transition at bar 142. The conductor's eyes.

body.speaker: Hartwig
body.text: All real. None of them is the right thing to be afraid of.

body.speaker: Mia
body.text: What is?

body.speaker: Hartwig
body.text: That the orchestra plays without you, and you cannot tell. The young player who is not yet in the orchestra plays beside it for two minutes before she realises she is alone. That is what I have seen ruin debuts. Not wrong notes. Not nerves. Disconnection.

body.speaker: Mia
body.text: How do I avoid that?

body.speaker: Hartwig
body.text: Breathe with the cello on your left. Not with yourself. Breathe with the cello. The cello cannot get nervous about you. If you breathe with her, you cannot drift.

body.speaker: Mia
body.text: Ana? The cello next to me is Ana?

body.speaker: Hartwig
body.text: Yes. She has played for thirty-one years. She has done this with eight first violins before you. She will breathe with you whether you breathe with her or not. Make it deliberate.

body.speaker: Mia
body.text: Breathe with Ana.

body.speaker: Hartwig
body.text: One more thing.

body.speaker: Mia
body.text: Please.

body.speaker: Hartwig
body.text: At the end, do not stand up first. The first violin who stands up first looks hungry. Stand up after the principal cellist. Always after.

body.speaker: Mia
body.text: Why?

body.speaker: Hartwig
body.text: Because you are twenty-four and the seat is yours for the next thirty years if you do not look hungry tonight.

body.speaker: Mia
body.text: … Maestro.

body.speaker: Hartwig
body.text: Go. They are calling.

body.speaker: Mia
body.text: Will you come tomorrow?

body.speaker: Hartwig
body.text: No. I came once. That is how it should be. Now you remember the advice without me in the room.

annotations.meaningVi: buổi ra mắt (lần đầu biểu diễn)
(âm nhạc) bè trưởng — người dẫn của một bè trong dàn nhạc

grammarNotes.title: First conditional + imperative for instructional advice
grammarNotes.bodyVi: B1 thường nối điều kiện loại 1 với mệnh lệnh để đưa lời khuyên 'nếu… thì…'. Trong bài: 'If you breathe with her, you cannot drift.' (điều kiện + kết quả) 'Breathe with the cello on your left.' (mệnh lệnh trước) 'Stand up after the principal cellist.' (mệnh lệnh). 'If you do not look hungry tonight, the seat is yours for thirty years.' (kết quả lâu dài). Cấu trúc này cho phép trao kinh nghiệm gọn, có hành động cụ thể.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: B1 often pairs first conditionals with imperatives to give 'if… then…' advice. The dialogue: 'If you breathe with her, you cannot drift.' (condition + result) 'Breathe with the cello on your left.' (imperative) 'Stand up after the principal cellist.' (imperative). 'If you do not look hungry tonight, the seat is yours for thirty years.' (long-term consequence). The pattern packs experience into concrete actions.

translationVi:
Hartwig: Mia. Hai phút nữa. Họ sẽ gọi em.
Mia: Maestro, thầy đã đến.
Hartwig: Tôi đến. Tôi không đứng hậu trường ba năm rồi.
Mia: Em sợ.
Hartwig: Tốt. Kể tôi nghe em sợ gì.
Mia: Quên mình đang ở đâu. Đoạn chuyển ở ô nhịp 142. Mắt của nhạc trưởng.
Hartwig: Tất cả đều thật. Không cái nào là cái đúng để sợ.
Mia: Cái gì đúng?
Hartwig: Sợ rằng dàn nhạc chơi mà không có em, và em không nhận ra. Nghệ sĩ trẻ chưa thực sự ở trong dàn nhạc chơi 'bên cạnh' nó hai phút trước khi nhận ra mình đang một mình. Đó là cái tôi đã thấy phá hỏng các buổi ra mắt. Không phải sai nốt. Không phải hồi hộp. Là sự mất kết nối.
Mia: Em tránh thế nào?
Hartwig: Thở cùng cây cello bên trái em. Không phải thở cùng chính em. Thở cùng cello. Cello không hồi hộp về em. Nếu em thở cùng nó, em không trôi đi được.
Mia: Ana? Cây cello bên em là chị Ana?
Hartwig: Đúng. Chị ấy chơi ba mươi mốt năm rồi. Chị đã làm việc này với tám violin một trước em. Chị sẽ thở cùng em dù em có thở cùng chị hay không. Hãy làm điều đó có chủ ý.
Mia: Thở cùng Ana.
Hartwig: Một điều nữa.
Mia: Thầy nói đi.
Hartwig: Cuối buổi, đừng đứng lên đầu tiên. Người violin một đứng lên đầu trông 'đói'. Đứng lên sau bè trưởng cello. Luôn sau.
Mia: Sao thầy?
Hartwig: Vì em hai mươi tư và cái ghế là của em trong ba mươi năm tới nếu em không 'đói' tối nay.
Mia: … Maestro.
Hartwig: Đi. Họ gọi rồi.
Mia: Mai thầy có đến không?
Hartwig: Không. Tôi đã đến một lần. Thế là đúng. Bây giờ em nhớ lời khuyên mà tôi không có trong phòng.

questions.prompt: How long has Hartwig been away from backstage?
questions.explanation: 'I have not been backstage in three years.'

questions.prompt: What does Mia say she is afraid of?
options: Forgetting where she is, the transition at bar 142, the conductor's eyes

questions.prompt: What does Hartwig say IS the right thing to be afraid of?
options: Disconnection — playing beside the orchestra without realising she is alone.
Breathe with the cello on her left, not with herself.
The cello on Mia's left — has played thirty-one years, with eight first violins before Mia

questions.prompt: What is Hartwig's second piece of advice?
options: Do not stand up first at the end — stand up after the principal cellist.
The first violin who stands up first looks hungry; the seat is hers for thirty years if she does not look hungry tonight.
It is rude to the audience.

questions.prompt: Does he plan to come to tomorrow's concert?
options: No — he came once; now she remembers the advice without him in the room.

questions.prompt: What does Hartwig say is the real risk for young players?
options: Disconnection — not nerves or wrong notes.
questions.hint: Look for the line 'not… not… disconnection.'

options: Appearing eager for attention or applause — which would damage her standing in the orchestra.
questions.hint: Look at the context with 'the seat is yours.'

questions.prompt: Which of these is the dialogue NOT claiming?
options: That the right preparation is about preventing wrong notes.
That a more experienced player can carry a younger one through.
That standing order at the end signals position.
That coaching can take place in two minutes.
questions.explanation: Hartwig explicitly says wrong notes are NOT the main risk.
questions.hint: Look at what Hartwig rules out.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the dialogue?
options: Surviving a high-stakes debut depends less on personal nerves than on staying physically linked to one experienced colleague — and on managing how one appears at the end.
questions.explanation: Hartwig's two-part advice: breathe with Ana; do not look hungry.
questions.hint: Notice that both pieces of advice are about relationship, not technique.

cloze.template:
Hartwig: Mia. Two minutes. They will call you.
Mia: Maestro, you came.
Hartwig: I came. I have not been {{b1}} in three years.
Mia: I am terrified.
Hartwig: Good. Tell me what you are afraid of.
Mia: Forgetting where I am. The transition at bar {{b2}}. The conductor's eyes.
Hartwig: All real. None of them is the right thing to be afraid of.
Mia: What is?
Hartwig: That the orchestra plays without you, and you cannot tell. The young player who is not yet in the orchestra plays beside it for two minutes before she realises she is alone. That is what I have seen ruin debuts. Not wrong notes. Not nerves. {{b3}}.
Mia: How do I avoid that?
Hartwig: {{b4}} with the cello on your left. Not with yourself. Breathe with the cello. The cello cannot get nervous about you. If you breathe with her, you cannot {{b5}}.
Mia: Ana? The cello next to me is Ana?
Hartwig: Yes. She has played for thirty-{{b6}} years. She has done this with eight first violins before you. She will breathe with you whether you breathe with her or not. Make it {{b7}}.
Mia: Breathe with Ana.
Hartwig: One more thing.
Mia: Please.
Hartwig: At the end, do not stand up first. The first violin who stands up first looks {{b8}}. Stand up after the principal cellist. Always after.
Mia: Why?
Hartwig: Because you are twenty-four and the seat is yours for the next {{b9}} years if you do not look hungry tonight.
Mia: … Maestro.
Hartwig: Go. They are calling.
Mia: Will you come tomorrow?
Hartwig: No. I came once. That is how it should be. Now you remember the advice without me in the {{b10}}.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Hartwig tells Mia to breathe with the cello on her left — to relocate her safety from herself to an experienced colleague. Is there a high-stakes moment in your own work where you have been relying only on yourself? Who is the 'Ana' you could breathe with instead?
